---
title: "Methodological considerations in assessing heterogeneity"
suppress-bibliography: true
---

In the present study, we used k-means clustering to assess heterogeneity among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes. K-means clustering is an unsupervised statistical method, which implies that the aim of the analysis is to identify distinct subgroups with different characteristics rather than predicting outcomes as in supervised methods (i.e regression models) [@james2021]. The method is widely used in prediabetes and type 2 diabetes research. [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025; @onthoni2025; @li2024]. In the following section, the process of the k-means algorithm will be briefly described, followed by a discussion of the limitations of the method related to the present study. Finally an alternative phenotyping method, the latent class trajectory analysis (LCTA) will be presented.

## The k-means clustering algorithm

The K-means clustering algorithm is an iterative process that aims to minimize the total variation within the clusters (compactness), and thereby maximizing the variation between clusters (separation). The algorithm is initialized by choosing the amount of clusters, *k,* desired for the specific analysis. The centers of the clusters, called centroids, will be randomly placed in the multidimensional space, followed by an assignment process where every data point is labelled to the closest centroid. Next, the centroids are relocated to the mean position of all data points assigned to that cluster. Finally, the data points are reassigned to new clusters, and this process is repeated until the centroid and assignments no longer change [@james2021; @differen18].

## Limitations of k-means clustering and cluster validation

Despite being a simple and widely used clustering algorithm [@james2021], k-means clustering has several limitations that need to be addressed [@kmeans2017]. Firstly, the use of means to calculate cluster centers makes k-means clustering sensitive to outliers, where extreme values might shift the centers disproportionally [@khan2024]. In the present analysis, the impact of outliers was reduced through logarithmic transformation of right-skewed variables prior to clustering.

<!-- Furthermore, a sensitivity analysis using the k-medoids algorithm was performed and compared to the solution of the k-means algorithm. K-medoids calculates the centers of the clusters based on medians instead of means, which makes it more robust to outliers [@heidari2024]. The results, showed in the appendix table B-1, indicates that the two solutions do not differ substantially and that the outliers in the OGTT-data did not have great effet on the placement of the cluster centers, justifying the use of the more simpler k-means algorithm in this study. -->

Secondly, the algorithm requires the analyst to specify the number of clusters in advance. To determine the optimal number of clusters in this study, two complementary approaches, the NbClust package and the silhouette method were applied. Finally and most important, k-means clustering is a hard-clustering method that assigns every observation to exactly one cluster, no matter how well that observation fits in its assigned cluster. This means that individuals near the boundaries of two clusters are treated the same as those close to the centers of the cluster. This limitation has direct implications for how the biological meaning of the subgroups is understood and will be discussed further below.

To quantify this uncertainty, the silhouette method was further applied to analyze how well each observation fits into their assigned cluster [@kaufman1990]. The silhouette width for observation *i* is calculated as:

$${S(i)} =  \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

Where a(i) is the mean distance to every other point in the same cluster and b(i) the mean distance to every other point in the nearest neighboring cluster. As shown in appendix figure B-1**,** the distribution of individual silhouette widths varies considerably both within and between clusters. While most of the observations have positive silhouette widths, a considerable proportion of cluster 3 (n = 72, 14.9%) have negative values, indicating that these individuals are closer to another cluster than to their own **(**Appendix table B-2)**.** Consequently, cluster 3 also has the lowest average silhouette width of 0.14, with cluster 1 having the highest value of 0.26 (Appendix table B-2). Importantly, the k-means algorithm assigns all observations with equal certainty, regardless of their silhouette width. Individuals with low or even negative silhouette widths may represent phenotypes overlap between clusters and do not fit into one single group [@wagner2026]. This is consistent with findings in diabetes phenotyping research, where most individuals have intermediate characteristics related to multiple subtypes [@wesolowska-andersen2022; @deutsch2022]. Since k-means does not provide any probabilistic measure of cluster assignments, this uncertainty is not quantified in subsequent analyses. Associations between cluster membership and measures of fat distribution, as presented in this paper, should therefore be interpreted with this in mind. This is particularly relevant for cluster 3, which has the largest proportion of negative silhouette widths.

## Latent class trajectory analysis as an alternative to k-means clustering

In this study glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon were used as input variables to k-means clustering. The five variables were derived from the OGTT, where each variable was measured at three time points (0, 30 and 120 minutes). K-means clustering is not suited to handle time-series data, and therefore does not account for possible complex, time-dependent correlations between measurements [@giordano2021]. To address this limitation, we reduced the complexity of different time point measurements to summary measures. Despite summary measures derived from the OGTT being widely used in previous studies [@wagner2026; @stroebel2025; @onthoni2025; @li2024], recent studies have identified distinct subgroups among individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes with different glucose patterns during the OGTT [@hulman2017; @hulman2018; @hulman2018a]. The method used in these studies, the latent class trajectory analysis (LCTA), is a novel approach to phenotyping on time-dependent data. In the following section, LCTA will be explained and discussed as an alternative to k-means clustering when using OGTT data.

The LCTA algorithm works by modelling the full trajectory of dynamic, longitudinal data [@proust-lima2017], for example glucose response patterns during the OGTT, as described in the three studies by Hulman et al. [@hulman2017; @hulman2018; @hulman2018a]. This addresses the limitation of k-means clustering not handling time-series data and captures correlations between measurements at different time points. LCTA assumes that the observed trajectories arise from latent classes, each with their own distinct trajectory curve shape. As with k-means clustering, LCTA requires the user to specify the number of latent classes in advance, which can be determined with different statistical criteria, with the Bayesian Information Criteria (BIC) being often used [@schwarz1978; @thegrol]. Furthermore, it is a mixed-model that estimates the average group trajectory and the individual variation. Importantly, LCTA calculates the probabilities of every individual belonging to each latent class, rather than hard assignments without any uncertainty measures as in k-means clustering. This allows uncertainty in subgroup assignments to be quantified and further used in subsequent analyses [@proust-lima2017].

In the case of OGTT data, it is worth discussing what modelling trajectories during the OGTT potentially could add beyond the use of summary measures. Two of the studies by Hulman et al. [@hulman2017; @hulman2018] showed how the identified subgroups shared similar fasting and two-hour glucose concentrations, but had different glucose peak characteristics. These distinct glucose response curves were associated with pathophysiological differences, mainly driven by insulin sensitivity and secretion. Since AUC-glucose summarizes the entire glucose curve into a single value, it does not show the timing and shape of the glucose response, which are the characteristics presented in these studies. Subgroups with identical AUC-glucose may therefore differ in peak glucose and timing, reflecting differences in insulin secretion and sensitivity, which the AUC-measure cannot reveal.

Despite the advantage of LCTA being able to identify differences among subgroups that summary measures like the AUC cannot, the fact that LCTA only models one variable at a time is an important limitation in relation to the aim of this study. Modelling separate trajectories for each of the five variables would result in five independent subgroup solutions with no clear relation between them, making it difficult to identify phenotypes reflecting the interplay between glucose, insulin, GLP-1, GIP and glucagon responses. Since capturing this was central to the research aim, a multivariate clustering method was required, and k-means clustering meets this criterion, even though it comes with several limitations.

Despite its limitations, k-means was chosen as the appropriate method for the specific research question in this study given the multivariate structure of the data and the need for comparability with existing literature. The silhouette analysis provided important nuances to the hard-clustering limitation by quantifying membership uncertainty. In future work, LCTA could be applied to individual hormonal trajectories in addition to multivariate clustering. This would make it possible to study the temporal dynamics and multivariate patterns separately, since neither of the methods can capture both aspects.
